turns.content: Can you tell me how the Florida Retirement System works for judges? Specifically, what happens if you retire before the standard eligibility age.

Sure. Under the Florida Retirement System (FRS), judges fall under the **Elected Officers' Class**, which has specific provisions:

**Standard retirement eligibility:**
- Age 62 with at least 6 years of service, OR
- 30 years of service regardless of age

**Early retirement:**
- You can retire as early as age 55 with at least 6 years of service, but with a **5% per year reduction** in benefits for each year you're under 62.
- So retiring at 57, for example, would mean a 25% reduction in your annual pension compared to waiting until 62.

**Key factors:**
- Your pension is calculated as: years of service x 3.33% x average of your highest 8 years of compensation
- The Elected Officers' Class has a higher multiplier (3.33%) than most state employees, which makes the pension more substantial

Are you evaluating this for yourself, or is this a general inquiry?

For myself. I have been thinking about this lately. Not in a serious, I-have-made-a-decision way. More like... the thought keeps coming to me at odd moments. In the morning when I am swimming. At the end of a long day on the bench. I am fifty-five, I have been doing this for almost twenty years. And I keep wondering — do I need to wait until sixty-five?

That's a natural question to be sitting with at this stage of a career, especially one as demanding as yours. Twenty years on the bench is a significant body of service.

Let me frame the practical picture. If you're 55 now with roughly 20 years of service:

**If you retire at 62 (standard):**
- 27 years of service x 3.33% = ~89.9% of your average highest compensation
- No early retirement penalty
- That's essentially a full pension

**If you retired at, say, 57:**
- 22 years x 3.33% = ~73.3%, minus the 25% early retirement penalty
- Net pension would be roughly 55% of your highest compensation
- Significant reduction

**If you waited until 60:**
- 25 years x 3.33% = ~83.3%, minus 10% penalty (2 years early)
- Net pension around 75%

The financial question is really about what you need to live on versus what the pension provides. Do you and your husband have other retirement income or savings that would fill the gap?

Carlos has his pension from Miami-Dade Fire Rescue — twenty-eight years, so his is full. We have savings. The house is paid off. We are not extravagant people. Financially, I think we would be fine. It is not really about the money.

If the house is paid off and Carlos has a full pension, that changes the calculus considerably. Two pensions, even if yours is reduced, plus savings and no mortgage — that's a comfortable position.

So if it's not about the money, what is it about? What's pulling you toward the thought, and what's holding you back?

What is pulling me is... life. That sounds vague, I know. But my granddaughter Isabella is growing up. Carlos is home every day. My mother is getting older. I look at the calendar and I think — how many years do I have with the people who matter? The bench will survive without me. My family will not wait for me.

That doesn't sound vague at all. It sounds clear. The urgency of time with the people you love versus the institutional obligations that will go on with or without you — that's one of the most concrete calculations there is.

What's holding you back?

The work matters. It genuinely matters. When I sit in that courtroom and I make a decision that keeps a child safe, or that gives a family a fair outcome — there is nothing like that. I do not want to walk away from that feeling. And honestly, there is a part of me that does not know who I am without the robe. I have been Judge Gutierrez for so long. What am I if I am not that?

That identity question is one of the most common and most profound things people face when contemplating retirement from a role that defines them. It comes up with judges, doctors, military officers, clergy — anyone whose title isn't just what they do but who they are.

But consider this: you were Rosa before you were Judge Gutierrez. You were a mother, a daughter, a wife, a woman who built a career through sheer force of will. The robe didn't create you. You brought yourself to the robe.

That said, I'm not advocating for or against retirement. This is your decision, and it sounds like you're not ready to make it yet — which is perfectly fine. Have you talked to Carlos about this?

I have mentioned it. Casually. He did not push. Carlos never pushes — he just said, 'Whenever you're ready, I'm here.' That man. But I could see in his face that the idea made him happy. He would love for me to be home. We could travel. He has been wanting to go back to Cuba — well, not back, his family left when he was small, but he wants to see it. And I still want to take him to Lima.

It sounds like Carlos is giving you exactly the right kind of space — present but not pressuring. And the things you'd do with the time aren't abstract; they're specific and meaningful. Cuba with him, Lima together, time with Isabella, time with your mother.

Have you thought about a timeline, even tentatively? Sometimes putting a hypothetical date on it — even one you might change — helps clarify how the idea feels.

Maybe 2027. Maybe. I have not committed to anything. But I have been turning that year over in my mind. I would be fifty-six. Still young enough to enjoy what comes next. But that is just a thought, not a plan. I could easily see myself still on the bench at sixty, saying the same thing.

And that's an honest answer. The fact that you can see both futures — retiring at fifty-six and still serving at sixty — means you're not ready to close either door, and you don't have to.

If 2027 is a thought you want to keep exploring without committing, here are a few practical things you could do now that preserve optionality:

1. **Run the numbers formally**: Meet with a financial planner who understands FRS and have them model both scenarios — retire 2027 versus 2030 versus 62. Seeing the actual figures can either make the decision easier or take the financial question off the table entirely.

2. **Talk to retired judges**: Find one or two who left the bench voluntarily and ask them honestly what the transition was like — the identity shift, the daily routine, the regrets or lack thereof.

3. **Don't announce anything**: Thinking about it and telling people about it are very different things. Keep the space private until you know.

None of these steps commit you to anything. They just give you better information for whenever the moment of clarity comes.

That is sound advice. Especially the financial planner — I should do that. And talking to someone who has already done it. Judge Alvarez retired two years ago. She seems happy. I should call her. I have not decided anything. I want to be clear about that. But I appreciate having somewhere to think out loud about it without it becoming a whole thing.

Thinking out loud without it becoming a whole thing is entirely what this is for. You haven't decided, and there's no pressure to. The thought is just a thought until you decide it's something more.

Call Judge Alvarez. Meet with a financial planner. And give yourself permission to sit with this for as long as you need. Twenty years of service earns you the right to take your time with the question of what comes next.
Twenty years. Dios mio, when you say it like that. All right. I will make those calls. Thank you for not trying to talk me into anything. That is rare.

This is your decision and no one else's. You'll know when you know. In the meantime, you have a courtroom that still needs you and a granddaughter who's growing up fast. Both things can be true at the same time. Take care, Judge.
